**Title:** Make Your Laravel App Agent-Ready with WebMCP: A Hands-On Tutorial

**Excerpt:** Google I/O 2026 made the agentic web official. WebMCP lets AI agents interact with your Laravel site through structured tools instead of brittle screen scraping. Here’s a complete, reproducible guide to implementing it today.

**Slug:** laravel-webmcp-agent-ready-tutorial

**Content:**

### Introduction

At Google I/O 2026, Chrome engineers unveiled a vision for the "agentic web." The centerpiece is **WebMCP** — a proposed W3C standard that lets you expose your website's functionality as structured tools that AI agents can discover and invoke directly. No more fragile screenshot-and-scroll automation. Agents can now fill out forms, call JavaScript functions, and retrieve structured data from your Laravel app as if they were using a native API.

For Laravel developers, this is a big deal. You already build robust backends. WebMCP lets you reuse your existing frontend logic — Blade forms, Livewire components, Alpine.js handlers — and make them callable by browser-integrated agents. The best part? It works as progressive enhancement: human visitors see nothing different, but agents get a machine-readable interface.

In this tutorial, you will:
- Understand the difference between WebMCP and backend MCP servers.
- Add the cross-browser polyfill to a Laravel app.
- Turn a standard Blade search form into an agent-ready tool using the declarative API.
- Register a dynamic JavaScript tool for AJAX actions using the programmatic API.
- Test everything with Chrome's Model Context Tool Inspector.
- Apply security best practices (CSRF, human-in-the-loop, HTTPS).

### What You Need

- A Laravel 11+ project (the code works on Laravel 10+ too).
- Chrome 146+ with the **Experimental Web Platform features** flag enabled, *or* any modern browser plus the MCP-B polyfill.
- The [Model Context Tool Inspector](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/model-context-tool-inspector) Chrome extension (optional but recommended).
- An HTTPS environment for testing (`valet secure`, `ngrok`, or a staging server). `navigator.modelContext` requires a SecureContext and will not work over HTTP.

> **Note on timing:** WebMCP is in W3C Community Group status. Chrome 146 ships with a DevTrial behind a flag, and the MCP-B polyfill provides fallback support for other browsers. The specification was last updated on February 12, 2026.

### WebMCP vs. Backend MCP: Why Both Matter

If you have been following Laravel's AI tooling, you know Laravel 13 ships with a first-party [MCP server package](https://laravel.com/docs/13.x/mcp). That is a **backend** integration: an AI agent talks to your Laravel API over HTTP, bypassing the browser entirely.

WebMCP is the **client-side** complement. The agent runs inside the user's browser (or a browser extension) and invokes tools that live on the current page. This means:
- **Shared context:** The agent sees the same DOM, session cookies, and CSRF tokens as the user.
- **No duplicate API:** You reuse your existing form endpoints and frontend JavaScript.
- **Human-in-the-loop:** By default, the browser focuses the form and waits for the user to click submit before any state changes.

Backend MCP is great for headless automation. WebMCP is great for interactive web apps where the user and agent cooperate.

### Step 1: Add the Polyfill

Native WebMCP support is still rolling out. To make your tools work for visitors on Firefox, Safari, or older Chrome versions, add the MCP-B polyfill to your main layout.

Open `resources/views/layouts/app.blade.php` (or your base layout) and add this before the closing `</body>` tag:

```blade
<script src="https://unpkg.com/@mcp-b/global@latest/dist/index.iife.js"></script>
```

The polyfill detects native support and steps aside automatically. When Chrome ships WebMCP without flags, you can remove this line and nothing else changes.

### Step 2: Make a Blade Form Agent-Ready (Declarative API)

The fastest way to expose functionality is the **declarative API**. You add three HTML attributes to any form, and the browser automatically compiles the inputs into a JSON schema that the agent understands.

Imagine you have a product search form in `resources/views/products/index.blade.php`:

```blade
<form action="{{ route('products.search') }}" method="GET">
    <input name="query" type="text" required placeholder="Search products..." />
    <select name="category">
        <option value="all">All</option>
        <option value="electronics">Electronics</option>
        <option value="clothing">Clothing</option>
    </select>
    <input name="max_price" type="number" placeholder="Max price" />
    <button type="submit">Search</button>
</form>
```

To make this agent-ready, add `toolname`, `tooldescription`, and `toolparamdescription` attributes:

```blade

    <select name="category"
            toolparamdescription="Product category to filter results">

summary:
**Content Mode: Hands-on Tutorial**

**Topic: Building an AI Agent from Scratch in Python**

**Title: Building an AI Agent from Scratch in Python: A Hands-on Guide**

---

### Introduction

Creating an AI agent from scratch can seem daunting, but with Python and a clear understanding of the components involved, you can build a simple yet functional AI agent. This tutorial will guide you through the process of creating an AI agent using Python, focusing on understanding each component's role and how they interact.

### Prerequisites

Before we begin, ensure you have the following:
- Python 3.8 or higher installed on your machine.
- Basic knowledge of Python programming.
- A text editor or IDE like VSCode or PyCharm.
- An account with an LLM API provider like Anthropic or OpenAI.

### Project Structure

Here's the structure of the project we'll be building:

```
ai_agent/
|-- .env
|-- agent.py
|-- requirements.txt
```

### Step 1: Setting Up the Environment

First, create a virtual environment and install the necessary libraries. Open your terminal and run:

```bash
python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows use `venv\Scripts\activate`
pip install anthropic python-dotenv
```

Create a `requirements.txt` file to keep track of dependencies:

```
anthropic==0.69.0
python-dotenv==0.19.2
```

### Step 2: Implementing the AI Agent

Create a file named `agent.py`. This file will contain the main logic of our AI agent.

#### 2.1: LLM and Instructions

The core of our AI agent is a Large Language Model (LLM). We'll use Anthropic's Claude 4 Sonnet for this tutorial. Start by importing the necessary libraries and setting up the LLM client:

```python
import anthropic
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

class Agent:
    """A simple AI agent that can answer questions"""

    def __init__(self):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"))
        self.model = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
        self.system_message = "You are a helpful assistant that breaks down problems into steps and solves them systematically."

    def chat(self, message):
        """Process a user message and return a response"""
        response = self.client.messages.create(
            model=self.model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=self.system_message,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": message}],
            temperature=0.1,
        )
        return response
```

#### 2.2: Conversation Memory

To handle conversation memory, we'll store previous interactions to provide context in ongoing conversations. Update the `Agent` class:

```python
class Agent:
    def __init__(self):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"))
        self.model = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
        self.system_message = "You are a helpful assistant that breaks down problems into steps and solves them systematically."
        self.conversation_history = []

    def chat(self, message):
        self.conversation_history.append({"role": "user", "content": message})
        response = self.client.messages.create(
            model=self.model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=self.system_message,
            messages=self.conversation_history,
            temperature=0.1,
        )
        self.conversation_history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content[0].text})
        return response
```

### Step 3: Testing the AI Agent

Let's test our AI agent. In the same `agent.py` file, add the following code to test:

```python
if __name__ == "__main__":
    agent = Agent()
    response = agent.chat("I have 4 apples. How many do you have?")
    print(response.content[0].text)

    response = agent.chat("I ate 1 apple. How many are left?")
    print(response.content[0].text)
```

### Common Errors and Fixes

- **API Key Error**: Ensure your `.env` file contains the correct `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.
- **Environment Setup**: If you encounter issues with the virtual environment, ensure it's activated before running any Python scripts.
- **Response Handling**: If the agent's responses are not as expected, check the conversation history logic.

### Conclusion

Congratulations! You've built a basic AI agent from scratch in Python. This agent can process simple conversations and maintain context across interactions. For further enhancements, consider adding more sophisticated memory management or integrating additional tools for the agent to use.

### Sources

- [Building an AI agent from scratch in Python - Leonie Monigatti](https://www.leoniemonigatti.com/blog/ai-agent-from-scratch-in-python.html)
- [Building AI Agents in Python with Pydantic AI - MachineLearningMastery.com](https://machinelearningmastery.com/building-ai-agents-in-python-with-pydantic-ai/)

### Title: Exploring Laravel 10's New Process Facade: A Hands-On Guide

---

Laravel 10 has introduced several exciting features, but one that stands out for developers is the new Process facade. Released in February 2023, this feature simplifies the way we handle external processes within Laravel applications. If you've ever needed to interact with command-line scripts or external processes, this update is a game changer.

#### Why the Process Facade Matters

The Process facade in Laravel 10 allows developers to run and manage external processes directly from their Laravel applications. This is particularly useful for tasks that require command-line interactions, such as running shell scripts, managing server processes, or executing build tools.

With the Process facade, you can now handle these tasks more elegantly and with less boilerplate code. It provides a fluent interface to start, monitor, and control external processes, making it easier to integrate such tasks into your Laravel application.

#### Setting Up Laravel 10

Before we dive into using the Process facade, ensure that your environment is set up with Laravel 10 and PHP 8.1 or higher. If you're upgrading from an earlier version, follow the [Laravel Upgrade Guide](https://laravel.com/docs/10.x/upgrade).

#### Building a Simple Process Manager

Let's build a simple Laravel application that uses the Process facade to interact with the command line. We'll create a route that lists the contents of a directory and returns the output.

##### Step 1: Create a New Laravel Project

First, create a new Laravel project:

```bash
composer create-project --prefer-dist laravel/laravel process-manager
cd process-manager
```

##### Step 2: Define a Route

Open `routes/web.php` and define a new route that uses the Process facade:

```php
use Illuminate\Support\Facades\Route;
use Illuminate\Support\Facades\Process;

Route::get('/list-directory', function () {
    $result = Process::run('ls -la');

    if ($result->successful()) {
        return nl2br($result->output());
    }

    return $result->errorOutput();
});
```

##### Step 3: Test the Route

Start the Laravel development server and test the new route:

```bash
php artisan serve
```

Visit `http://localhost:8000/list-directory` in your browser. You should see the contents of your project directory listed.

#### Understanding the Process Facade

The Process facade provides several methods to interact with processes:

- **run**: Executes a command and returns a `ProcessResult` object.
- **successful**: Checks if the process exited successfully.
- **failed**: Checks if the process failed.
- **exitCode**: Retrieves the process exit code.
- **output**: Gets the standard output.
- **errorOutput**: Gets the error output.

#### Handling Errors

If a command fails, you can handle it gracefully:

```php
$result = Process::run('invalid-command');

if ($result->failed()) {
    return response()->json([
        'error' => 'Command failed',
        'details' => $result->errorOutput()
    ], 500);
}
```

#### Advanced Usage

The Process facade also supports asynchronous processes, process pools, and rich testing features via `fake()`. This makes it a versatile tool for managing complex workflows.

#### Conclusion

The new Process facade in Laravel 10 makes it easier than ever to work with external processes. Whether you're running simple shell commands or managing complex workflows, this feature provides a powerful and flexible solution.

For more hands-on engineering content, follow us and explore the full potential of Laravel 10.

#### Sources

- [Laravel 10 Release Notes](https://laravel.com/docs/10.x/releases)
- [Laravel 10 Features & Updates](https://redberry.international/laravel-10-features-and-updates/)

---

This tutorial provides a practical introduction to using the Process facade in Laravel 10. If you're looking to integrate command-line interactions into your Laravel applications, this is a feature you won't want to miss.
